Thanks for the contribution @ChandanChainani!

I'm a bit fussy about patching framework fundamentals, and especially about touching header casing. Manipulating header casing keeps causing us incredible headaches, so I would prefer not to make this broad of a patch. Moreover, technically the `Headers` does not allow boolean values in the first place.

It seems better to me check for the root of the problem, which seems to be here in moto: https://github.com/spulec/moto/blob/3118090fdc38de64e0a7aef969b3b1992c1703f5/moto/s3/models.py#L248-L250 I think we should just change the line to `str(self.bucket_key_enabled).lower()` there.

Moving forward this shouldn't be a problem at all, since we are currently migrating s3 to the new framework, which will serialize the response types properly into an HTTP response, which covers boolean to string conversions. But for now, it seems moto is the best place to make this change.

I think the solver options could be handled in a better way. A suggestion:

1. pop all the command line options from the `solver_options` dict with defaults. In the form `parallel = solver_options.pop(parallel, False)`. All of these are added to the command line (later)
2. Set a defaults dict for the `option_file_options` which include the options which you have in line 670-674. 
3. Update the `option_file_options` dict with the remaining `solver_options`, using `option_file_options.update(solver_options)` 
4. Convert the `option_file_options` to text form and write it to the file. 

In this way, one can easily access command line options and option-file options. What do you think?

Not exhaustive, but some updates to docstrings based on flake8-docstrings linting.  We can fix these here, or turn off some of the doctring feedback in the code.  In general, here's our errors in this area:
```
D200 One-line docstring should fit on one line with quotes
D202 No blank lines allowed after function docstring
D204 1 blank line required after class docstring
D205 1 blank line required between summary line and description
D210 No whitespaces allowed surrounding docstring text
D400 First line should end with a period
D401 First line should be in imperative mood
```
Should be good to go after the super-call has been cleaned up.

Thanks! Just a couple of non-functional suggestions.

Functionally, I noticed there's something a little unfortunate happening, which is that [RRs in an RRSet cannot have different TTLs](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2181#section-5.2).

If the zone's start state is:

    _acme-challenge.example.com. TXT 90 "preexisting"

the zone after `add_txt_record` would ideally keep the 90 TTL on the original RR, so we can restore it later:

    _acme-challenge.example.com. TXT 90 "preexisting"
    _acme-challenge.example.com. TXT 60 "the record we added"

but instead, it's ending up as:

    _acme-challenge.example.com. TXT 60 "preexisting"
    _acme-challenge.example.com. TXT 60 "the record we added"

and after `del_txt_record`:

    _acme-challenge.example.com. TXT 60 "preexisting"

The upshot of this is that the original TTL of `90` gets lost forever. The only way we could avoid this would be to reuse the TTL of the preexisting RR, instead of using `self.ttl`/`60`.

I'm not sure whether we should do that, or just live with the fact that we're going to overwrite the TTLs every time. I'd be curious to hear your opinion and we can gather some other opinions as well if necessary.

Could you update the development installation instructions to explain how to set up pre-commit?

I've also found that people can sometimes find pre-commit frustrating when hooks cannot automatically fix the issues they discover. For example, `black` works great as a pre-commit step for most people because it automatically fixes any stylistic mistakes, but `flake8` can be annoying because it reports the error without fixing it and will block you from committing until you do. If the commit is really just a WIP and you don't want to fix everything you have to know to go look at the pre-commit docs and figure out how to turn off its checks for that commit.

Pre-commit checks like `flake8` are fine to run in CI, but maybe not by default on ever commit a developer might make as they're writing code. Given this, it looks like pre-commit allows you to configure when hooks will run [using `stages`](https://pre-commit.com/#confining-hooks-to-run-at-certain-stages). Perhaps for those hooks which do not automatically correct mistakes they could be marked as `manual` and only run in CI.

On the whole, it's looking good, but I have a few requests. Most of them are about using centralized utilities, reducing the proliferation of type-lists that were in v1.

The type name for leaves of the type tree changed from PrimitiveType to NumpyType. As far as I can see, there is no `ak._v2.types.PrimitiveType`, so how are these tests working? Virtually every attempt to build a type would have to terminate on these leaf-nodes, so you ought to be getting

```
AttributeError: module 'awkward._v2.types' has no attribute 'PrimitiveType'
```

(I don't see a PrimitiveType being defined in this diff.)

Anyway, in an attempt to use terminology more consistently, the NumpyArray has NumpyForm and NumpyType. We still use the word "primitive," but it refers to the _string_ naming the type in Forms. v1 and v2 have to be able to accept each others' Forms so that pickled data from each version can be used in the other (that was a goal, so that users could build work-arounds if they need to), and that means the JSON field name "primitive" in the Form can't be changed. (Otherwise, a better name _might_ be "dtype", though that would also be misleading because dtypes are Python objects, and this is a string. So it's just going to be "primitive".)

3. Could you add tests for the dataset? You can create a `Country211TestCase` in [this file](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/e65a857b5487a8493bc8a80a95d64d9f049de347/test/test_datasets.py#L2206). You basically only need to provide a minimal configuration as well as provide fake data. This works, but only for Python 3.10. Setting `distclass = BinaryDistribution` solves the

```bash
sed "s/Root-Is-Purelib: true/Root-Is-Purelib: false/" < tmp_WHEEL
```

problem we used to have! There's still the problem that this wheel is labeled as being Python version dependent when it isn't (since the compiled code is accessed through ctypes, not any compiled pybind11 bindings).

We don't want to reintroduce the unzipping, editing metadata by hand, rezipping to label it as Python version independent (that information needs to go into the `Tag` section of the `WHEEL` file inside the zipped whl). I don't know of a solution for that (@henryiii?). In the meantime, we can just extend the matrix over all Python versions: 7 CUDA versions × 5 Python versions = 35 wheel files, which is still just 11.5 MB.

Another request: during ordinary CI testing, let's just do the minimum CUDA version and the maximum CUDA version for _one_ version of Python (it can be 3.10), so only 2 GitHub Action VMs are expended on this. During deployment, all 35 jobs can run, and they should be defined in wheels.yml so that `upload_all` will wait for them and include them in the upload (though they'll be uploaded to the `awkward-cuda-kernels` package name, not the `awkward` package name).

Yep, this looks about right. A few comments:

1. It's probably better to use serialized JSON instead of checking individual fields, b/c it catches more changes as we make them. It's saved us a lot of times while tweaking parsers. Have you found the examples of this in the test suite?

2. This will need to be added to `__init__.py` to make it accessible.

3. In terms of JSON schema, I suppose this should try to match the docket schema as closely as possible. (You can find a gazillion examples of this in the docket parser tests.) By making it match, it *should* make our ingestion pipeline able to use existing code.

4. There are a handful of things we do to normalize content in the docket parser. Things like tweaking the case name to make it a bit normalized, cleaning out bad data, etc. For example, `lib.string_utils.harmonize` is usually applied to the case name, `lib.string_utils.clean_string` is a good one to clean up messy strings, and `lib.string_utils.convert_date_string` is a nice way to get dates from strings with a bit of robustness.

I think that's it for now. Looks basically right though, yes.

Hey thanks for the PR! I know the rest of my review is quite negative, but I genuinely appreciate the time and energy you've put into putting this together!

I don't like this change because it provides the wrong feedback to a not an insignificant group of users. If I (as a Python 3 only user) give Black an invalid file, the Python 3.X parse attempts will fail and then Black exits with a "Black attempted to parse python2 code, [...] please install typed_ast" error... which makes no sense to me as a Python 3 only user. Not only is this hiding the true problem: the input file isn't valid Python 3 code, the confused user, might try the advice to install `typed_ast` which 1) might not work or be a pain, and 2) doesn't actually the fix the issue.

Personally I'm open to pushing most if not all of the disruption to the users still using Black's Python 2 support. My idea would be that Black would need `--target-version py27` (configured automatically via magic or configured manually) to even attempt Python 2 parsing. Although this falls apart when Black is formatting multiple files at once (since someone is bound to have a mix of Python 2 and 3 files... of which our target-version logic can't really describe).

Since I personally never use `get_neighbors_by_distance`, I'm fine with any implementation. I just want to say that if the goal is to get a good number of neighbors, this algorithm is probably a bit too expensive, because it internally runs `_get_neighbors` possibly multiple times (which itself is a relatively expensive function). Besides, there's still no guarantee that you get the correct number of neighbors in the end, since `_get_neighbors` itself relies on `_get_boundary_layer_width`, which estimates the width required from the density. In total, what in my opinion makes the most sense is to estimate the number of atoms from the global density (or Voronoi, if you really want to be sure), i.e. it should be:

```
num_neighbors = (1+buffer)*length**3/volume_per_atom
```

And maybe it's up to the user to determine `buffer`, or you can set a ridiculously large number for `buffer`, since if the number of neighbors is too large, the tree search simply ends so you will probably not lose much computation time.

I'm just a random contributor, not @asottile , but I had some comments on your file type additions based on my experience.

With regard to your overall approach, it would simplify the implementation considerably, make it more flexible/general, and less brittle/special case, if you avoided implementing a bunch of bespoke logic just for parsing salt files (requiring any other extension added to `EXTENSIONS_NEED_SHEBANG_CHECK` to do the same, or silently not work properly). Instead, why not move the `salt` extension key/value as-is to the `EXTENSIONS_NEED_SHEBANG_CHECK` dict, add the appropriate interpreters to the interpreters file, add a function that returns whether the file needs a shebang check (e.g. via the first 3-4 lines of your existing new one), and tweak the execution flow to cut out the `else` on line 57 and change the `if` below to `if (not len(t) and executable) or needs_shebang_check(os.path.basename(path))`, so the shebang check runs on such files? This is also a little more efficient (the extra extension check only runs when the shebang isn't already known to be needed), matches the format of the existing `EXTENSIONS_NEED_BINARY_CHECK`, and avoids unnecessary bespoke code and data to retrieve extensions from tags and having to hardcode interpreters for each extension, and extensions for each interpreter. Plus, it completely avoids any of the codes the test coverage is complaining about.

Hi @Joshix-1,

this seems to be about invocation `ansi2html --partial`.  I'm not a big fan of this pull request as-is because:
- Use of `<style>` is not allowed outside of `<head>` according to https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/style .
- Current users of `--partial` would experience backwards-incompatible behavior
- Working around `--inline` is likely not worth the savings in 99% of environments today, besides purism. I'd be curious how you environment is different.
- (The CI is failing.)
- (There are no new related tests yet.)
- (There are seemingly unrelated changes to white space of the generated code in the PR.)

If this direction is of true importance, maybe adding a new mode argument `--partial-plus-style` would be more clear and backwards-compatible.  My personal vote would be to re-evaluate `--inline` first, though. What do you think?

Best, Sebastian

Today I looked closer at both the changes in this PR and also the preexisting code and a few things raised some flags.

I went back to check [the Figma spec](https://www.figma.com/file/uiZuCIqh2KYvBUfbCiJyyA/Hybrid-Learning?node-id=76%3A6747), and unfortunately it isn't explicit about how the grid layout within the card is supposed to work. This is something that future KDS reviews should hopefully help address.

Looking at it though, my impression was that the intent is something like this:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/146200859-a97ff6ac-1cde-41e6-986e-e4c352368015.png)

However the actual DOM structure looks like this:

![2021-12-15 08 45 59](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/146201031-ec2f4983-4748-41dc-add5-d00d8702c600.gif)

One serious user-facing consequence of this is that the whole bottom-half of the card looks like it should be clickable but is not. This will cause especially tablet users a ton of frustration:

![2021-12-15 09 00 57](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/146201197-c4ac1a10-ef0c-4875-afd5-6bf3cef136cf.gif)

I think the overall layout of this component needs to be cleaned up. If it would be helpful, I'd be happy to take this on in order to demonstrate how `KGrid` can be useful here.

Another round of benchmarks after the new commits. Benchmark removed CUDA test and only tested one thread. On the flip side, measurements are not running longer to reduce the noise.
benchmark script

```python import pathlib import pickle import torch from torch.utils import benchmark import functools from torchvision.prototype.transforms import functional as F description = "PR" # "main", "PR" def make_inputs(*, input_dtype, output_dtype, shape=(3, 512, 512)): if input_dtype.is_floating_point: image = torch.rand(shape, dtype=input_dtype) else: image = torch.randint(0, torch.iinfo(input_dtype).max + 1, shape, dtype=input_dtype) return image, output_dtype sub_labels_and_input_fns = [ ("float to float", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.float32, output_dtype=torch.float64)), ("float to int", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.float32, output_dtype=torch.uint8)), (" int to float", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.uint8, output_dtype=torch.float32)), (" int to int (down)", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.int32, output_dtype=torch.uint8)), (" int to int (up)", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.uint8, output_dtype=torch.int32)), ] timers = [ benchmark.Timer( stmt="convert_image_dtype(*inputs)", globals=dict( convert_image_dtype=F.convert_image_dtype, inputs=inputs_fn(), ), label="convert_image_dtype perf improvements", sub_label=sub_label, description=description, num_threads=1, ) for sub_label, inputs_fn in sub_labels_and_input_fns ] measurements = [timer.blocked_autorange(min_run_time=15) for timer in timers] with open(f"{description}.measurements", "wb") as fh: pickle.dump(measurements, fh) measurements = [] for file in pathlib.Path(".").glob("*.measurements"): with open(file, "rb") as fh: measurements.extend(pickle.load(fh)) comparison = benchmark.Compare(measurements) comparison.trim_significant_figures() comparison.print() ```

``` [- convert_image_dtype perf improvements --] | main | PR 1 threads: --------------------------------- float to float | 90 | 83 float to int | 380 | 380 int to float | 138 | 134 int to int (down) | 1100 | 402 int to int (up) | 127 | 92 Times are in microseconds (us). ``` - "float to float", "int to float", "int to int (up)" did not change from the last benchmarks and are still faster - "int to int (down)" now uses bit shifts and is 3x faster

Everything worked on my devbox!

In addition, I tried to test the functionality of fault tolerance by the following steps:
* Step 1: Install a detached actor in the Ray's namespace "detached_actor_ns"
* Step 2: Kill the GCS server process in the head Pod, and wait the head container to restart.
   `kubectl exec -it ${HEAD_POD} -- pkill gcs_server`
* Step 3: The detached actor needs tens of seconds to restore after the head Pod restarts.

Step 3 can access the detached actor, so the fault-tolerance feature works as expected. 

# One question about fault tolerance
[Ray v2 Architecture (Failure Model)](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1tBw9A4j62ruI5omIJbMxly-la5w4q_TjyJgJL_jN2fI/preview#heading=h.mduznt6qyr8a) mentions that "Ray worker nodes are designed to be homogeneous, so that any single node may be lost without bringing down the entire cluster. The current exception to this is the head node, since it hosts the GCS. In 2.0, we have added experimental support for GCS fault tolerance, which allows the GCS to be restarted while minimizing disturbance to the rest of the cluster.".

Hence, I expected that the entire cluster will be brought down after the `gcs_server` process is killed in Step 2. However, worker Pods were not impacted by the restart of the head Pod.

Maybe this fulfills the description " allows the GCS to be restarted while minimizing disturbance to the rest of the cluster."?

@architkulkarni do you know who is the expert of fault tolerance? Thank you!

So, we already do have read/write build caching set up for Bazel, using a GCS bucket: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/6e192f35f9700cdf0a11645edd795b619db973d0/ci/bazelrc#L27-L34

(The cache is only written to for submitted code: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/6e192f35f9700cdf0a11645edd795b619db973d0/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L53-L72) 

Could you elaborate a little about the current timeout errors you're seeing?

Bazel is pretty finicky about what you cache, and it's easy for over-broad caching to mess up the hermeticity guarantees, which is why we've tried to stick to the built-in caching support. To use `actions/cache` you'd need to set the `key` to something that avoids cache pollution. I'm also not sure `bazel-bin` is a path you'd want to cache, since AFAIK it contains built binaries, so a clean `bazel fetch` wouldn't actually write anything to `bazel-bin` anyway - it looks like fetching writes to this output path: https://bazel.build/docs/external#layout But again, if possible it's probably safer to try to tune the built-in caching rather than manually caching bits of the Bazel output tree.

I've reviewed this with the premise that is a module migration to work with RDM but is not the intention to leave the code 100% clean and fix previous problems (although many were done anyway). I tried to review as much as possible, but is very hard to make an in-depth review in a sensible time due to its size; so my comments are more on structure than actual code implementation.

Tiny rant, nothing personal:

I'm seeing this (very big PRs, I've done it too), and I think we are setting ourselves for failure. Big PRs stay very long in the review column since they are very intimidating. For future reference, it would be nice to have more self contained PRs.
- Black and other migrations
- Example app removal
- Actual code/functionality changes

Having all in one PR with 54 files and 2.2/2.7k changes is quite unmanagable and very time consuming to review. Half of the brain power goes to understand if the change was black formatting or actual code. We can review commits, but in this case there's 5 on the actual code so there's still a need to jump from commit to commit.

Honestly, even GitHub lags to handle a PR of this size 😂 

Comments apply in many places related to layers:

- We are importing `NoResultsFound` in some classes and in some we are not.
- There is an `api.py` module and others get an actual name, e.g. `receivers.py`, nonetheless many seem to be APIs. 
- A clear separation between whats a model and what's an API, and even maybe splitting some of those API classes.
- I'd suggest to have an `/api` folder and then tiny classes inside since it seems this module does not need an actual service layer but it can be okay with just an api to bridge services (in rdm-records) to the GH api.

Thanks for the PR.

Streamlink already implements the livestream.com plugin (which even got rewritten recently), so the current plugin implementation is pretty much unnecessary and also wrong/incomplete, so I'm not going to comment on the code after the first HTTP request.

Due to the embedding of 3rd party streaming sites on atptour.com, the plugin needs to be turned into a wrapper plugin which just calls `return self.session.streams(url)`.

Adding new plugins which are only wrappers however is always questionable, and it depends on whether the user can get the actual streaming URL from their web browser which could then alternatively be used as Streamlink's input URL. Adding wrapper plugins for external sites overloads Streamlink with useless plugins that (currently) slow down the session initialization, so we want to avoid this. It's also problematic from the perspective of plugin maintenance.

Since there doesn't seem to be a way though to get the actual stream URL from livestream.com via the ATPtour website, I guess adding a wrapper plugin in this case is okay.

----

The following diff should be enough for a wrapper plugin and it also fixes some other issues:

```diff

+$description Tennis tournaments organized by the Association of Tennis Professionals.
+$url atptour.com/en/atp-challenger-tour/challenger-tv
 $type live, vod
 """
 
@@ -9,55 +9,22 @@ import re
 
 from streamlink.plugin import Plugin, pluginmatcher
 from streamlink.plugin.api import validate
-from streamlink.stream.hls import HLSStream
 
 log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
 
 
 @pluginmatcher(re.compile(
-    r"https?://(?:www\.)?atptour.com/en/atp-challenger-tour/challenger-tv"
+    r"https?://(?:www\.)?atptour\.com/(?:en|es)/atp-challenger-tour/challenger-tv"
 ))
 class AtpChallengerTour(Plugin):
-    _re_window_config = re.compile(r""".*window.config\s*=\s*(?P<json>{.*?});""", re.DOTALL)
-
     def _get_streams(self):
         iframe_url = self.session.http.get(self.url, schema=validate.Schema(

-

+        if iframe_url:
+            return self.session.streams(iframe_url)
 
 
 __plugin__ = AtpChallengerTour
